title: Advanced Storage Planner Guide
summary: Understand average and peak retention pressure for multi-camera systems with duty cycle, bitrate, usable capacity, and peak behavior.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates average-state and peak-state retention using camera count, bitrate, recording duty, storage capacity, and peak multiplier assumptions.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when the basic storage number is not enough and the design needs to compare normal operation against heavier scene or peak ingest behavior.
bullets: Early video-storage design planning.
Expansion review or recorder replacement.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting.
Explaining storage, retention, and quality tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical video-storage planning terms.

items.label: Camera count
items.description: The number of cameras included in the advanced storage scenario.
items.whyItMatters: Camera count scales both average and peak ingest.
items.commonMistake: Testing storage margin on a partial camera count and applying it to the full site.

items.label: Average bitrate per camera (Mbps)
items.description: The typical bitrate expected for each camera during normal operation.
items.whyItMatters: Average bitrate establishes the baseline storage load.
items.commonMistake: Using a quiet test bitrate when the real site spends much of the day at higher complexity.

links.label: Estimate baseline bitrate
links.note: Use this if bitrate assumptions are still being built.

items.label: Motion / recording duty (%)
items.description: The percentage of time video is recorded or effectively stored.
items.whyItMatters: Duty cycle controls how much of each day contributes to storage use.
items.commonMistake: Assuming motion-based recording is always low duty regardless of the scene.

items.label: Peak multiplier (x)
items.description: The multiplier applied to represent heavier-than-average ingest behavior.
items.whyItMatters: Peak multiplier shows how storage margin behaves when bitrate or activity rises.
items.commonMistake: Only planning average-state retention and ignoring real-world peaks.

items.label: Usable storage (TB)
items.description: The capacity actually available for retained footage after overhead and redundancy.
items.whyItMatters: Usable storage determines how many days can be retained under average and peak conditions.
items.commonMistake: Entering raw disk capacity instead of usable capacity after RAID and reserves.

links.label: Validate usable RAID capacity
links.note: Use this when usable storage depends on RAID layout.

items.label: Target retention (days)
items.description: The retention goal used to judge whether the design has enough margin.
items.whyItMatters: Target retention is the benchmark for average and peak retention pressure.
items.commonMistake: Accepting average retention while peak retention falls below the required goal.

links.label: Review retention target
links.note: Use this to check the retention policy behind the target.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means average and peak retention remain above the target. Watch means peak or average pressure is close enough to review. Risk means the storage plan may fail the retention target under realistic load.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add capacity, reduce demand, adjust policy, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most video-storage planning problems come from treating bitrate, storage, retention, RAID, and failure behavior as separate decisions even though they shape the same final retention promise.
bullets: Sizing storage from one average bitrate without checking peak, motion, codec, and retention behavior.
Treating nominal capacity as usable capacity after RAID, filesystem, spare, and operational reserves.
Ignoring growth, camera additions, quality changes, and retention policy changes after day one.
Failing to document assumptions before the recorder, storage tier, or archive policy is selected.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, VMS platform limits, camera-specific behavior, storage vendor guidance, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Camera bitrate can vary significantly with scene motion, lighting, compression settings, and codec behavior.
Usable storage should account for redundancy, reserved space, filesystem overhead, and operational margin.
Retention targets should be validated against stakeholder expectations, evidence needs, and system operation.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, expand, and troubleshoot.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal design review, manufacturer validation, field testing, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual VMS, recorder, camera model, codec profile, storage platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for retention, archive access, failure behavior, and acceptable image quality.
Document assumptions before implementation so future changes can be evaluated against the original design intent.
